1: "Praise the LORD! How blessed is the one who fears the LORD and finds great joy in his commands!" — the beatitude that opens this paired acrostic (Ps 111 praises YHWH's character; Ps 112 describes the one who reflects it). The one who finds great joy in YHWH's commands is ultimately Christ himself, who said "my food is to do the will of him who sent me" (John 4:34) and "I have come to do your will, my God" (Hebrews 10:7, quoting Psalms 40:8). The beatitude of Psalms 112:1 is most fully realized in Jesus, the perfectly godly Man, and derivatively in all who are in him.
2: "His children will be a powerful presence in the land; the descendants of the upright will be blessed" — the generative blessing of the righteous man's lineage. Christ's "descendants" are his people, born not of blood but of the Spirit (John 1:13; Psalms 3:6). Isaiah 53:10: "he will see his offspring (zeraʿ) and prolong his days." The children of the righteous man in Psalms 112:2 are the spiritual children of Christ — the community born from his death (John 12:24: "unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit").
3: "Prosperity and riches fill his home, and his righteousness endures forever" — the lasting righteousness that fills the house. Second Corinthians Psalms 8:9: "you know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you through his poverty might become rich." The prosperity of Psalms 112:3 is ultimately Christ's eschatological inheritance shared with his people — the riches of the glory of the inheritance that the Spirit is the guarantee of (Ephesians 1:14, 18).
4: "Even in the dark, light breaks for the upright; he is gracious, compassionate, and righteous" — the Exodus 34:6 formula applied now to the godly person who reflects YHWH's character. Jesus says "I am the light of the world" (John 8:12; Psalms 9:5). The light that breaks in darkness for the upright is Christ himself — he is both the source of light and, in his incarnate person, the demonstration of gracious-compassionate-righteous character. The godly man of Psalms 112:4 is a type of Christ, the one who most fully embodies all three divine attributes.
5: "It goes well with the one who is generous in lending and manages his business with justice" — the just and generous person. More directly, Christ is the one who gives himself — "No one takes my life from me, but I lay it down of my own accord" (John 10:18). The generosity described in Psalms 112:5 finds its ultimate type in the self-giving of Christ who gives not money but himself for the poor in spirit.
6: "The righteous will never be shaken; they will be remembered forever" — the permanence of the righteous man's standing. Hebrews 12:28: "since we are receiving a kingdom that cannot be shaken, let us be thankful." The unshakeability of the righteous in Psalms 112:6 is derived from Christ's own unshakeability: the one who is "the same yesterday and today and forever" (Hebrews 13:8) is the anchor of the community's eternal standing. "I give them eternal life, and they shall never perish; no one will snatch them out of my hand" (John 10:28).
7: "He does not fear bad news; his heart is steady, trusting in the LORD" — the stillness of the trusting heart. Jesus in Gethsemane is the supreme example: even facing the cross, "not my will but yours" (Luke 22:42). The steadiness-in-trust of Psalms 112:7 is what Christ embodies in the garden; and through his steadiness, he becomes the ground of the community's steadiness. "Do not let your hearts be troubled. You believe in God; believe also in me" (John 14:1).
8: "His heart is steady; he will not fear. In the end he will see his enemies get what they deserve" — the unfearing heart that will witness final vindication. Hebrews 12:2: "For the joy set before him he endured the cross, scorning its shame, and sat down at the right hand of the throne of God." Christ's steadiness through crucifixion leads to the vindication of resurrection and the subjection of every enemy under his feet (Psalms 110:1; 1 Corinthians 15:25). The "in the end" of Psalms 112:8 is the eschatological triumph that Christ's resurrection guarantees.
9: "He gives freely to the poor; his righteousness endures forever; his honor is lifted high" — Paul quotes this directly in 2 Corinthians 9:9: "As it is written: 'He has scattered abroad his gifts to the poor; his righteousness endures forever'" (DIRECT CITATION). Paul applies Psalms 112:9 to the generous giving that reflects the gospel character. But the source of all this giving is Christ: "though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor" (2 Corinthians 8:9). The one who gives freely to the poor is ultimately Christ himself, and those who give generously participate in his character — the honor lifted high is the honor given to Christ at the right hand of the Father (Acts 2:33).
10: "The wicked see all this and seethe; they grind their teeth and waste away. What the wicked crave will come to nothing" — the gnashing-teeth response of the wicked to the righteous man's vindication. Acts 7:54: when Stephen describes the risen Christ at God's right hand, "they were furious and gnashed their teeth at him." The wicked seeing and seething at the godly man's vindication is the scene at Stephen's martyrdom — and at every moment when the truth of Christ's resurrection becomes undeniable to those who reject it. What they crave — the erasure of the righteous and his vindication — comes to nothing.
